coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of luminescent carbon dots and carbon-coated metal particles: Two birds with one stone
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Baohua
Yang, Qiao
Li, Zhonghao
Hao, Jingcheng
coredata.dc:description: A one-pot green synthesis of luminescent carbon dots and carbon-coated metal (Au, Pt, Pd) particles is proposed by using the vitamin B2 salt (riboflavin 5′-monophosphate sodium salt hydrate) as the carbon source and the reducing agent. The hydrothermal heating of B2 salt and metal salt solution simultaneously can result in water soluble carbon dots in supernatant and carbon-coated metal particles in the precipitate. The size and the luminescent properties of the as-prepared carbon dots are dependent on the concentration of the B2 salt in the synthesis. Moreover, the as-prepared carbon dots show both nitrogen and phosphorus doped characteristic, which is beneficial for their luminescent property. Besides the water soluble carbon dots recovered from the supernatant, there are also interesting carbon-coated metal particles recovered from the precipitates. Such metal-loaded carbon particles might have great potential applications in the catalysis field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Nanoparticles
Nanostructure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and carbon-coated metal particles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the as-prepared carbon dots. (b) The corresponding size distribution histogram.
FTIR spectrum and XPS spectra of the as-prepared carbon dots. FTIR spectrum (a), high resolution XPS spectra and the corresponding deconvoluted spectra for (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) N1s, (e) P2p.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared carbon dots, inset: photographs under daylight (left) and 365nm UV light (right). (b) Photoluminescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.
TEM images and the corresponding size distribution histograms of the as-prepared carbon dots obtained with the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt at 1:1 (a, b) and 1:50 (c, d).
Photoluminescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths for the as-prepared carbon dots with the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt at 1:1 (a) and 1:50 (b).

(a) XRD pattern of as-prepared carbon-coated Pt particles synthesized with the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt at 1:10. Low and higher magnification TEM images of as-prepared carbon-coated Pt particles synthesized with the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt at 1:1(b, c), 1:10 (d, e) and 1:50 (f).

(a) XRD pattern of carbon-coated Au and carbon-coated Pd particles synthesized with the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt at 1:10. Low and high magnification TEM images of carbon-coated Au with the mole ratio of gold salt to B2 salt at 1:1 (b, c), 1:10 (d), 1:50 (e) and carbon-coated Pd particles with the mole ratio of palladium salt to B2 salt at 1:1 (f), 1:10 (g) and 1:50 (h).

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of luminescent carbon dots and carbon-coated metal particles: Two birds with one stone

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Shandong University, Ministry of Education, Jinan 250100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A one-pot green synthesis strategy is proposed for the fabrication of luminescent carbon dots and carbon-coated metal particles.

A one-pot green synthesis of luminescent carbon dots and carbon-coated metal (Au, Pt, Pd) particles is proposed by using the vitamin B2 salt (riboflavin 5′-monophosphate sodium salt hydrate) as the carbon source and the reducing agent. The hydrothermal heating of B2 salt and metal salt solution simultaneously can result in water soluble carbon dots in supernatant and carbon-coated metal particles in the precipitate. The size and the luminescent properties of the as-prepared carbon dots are dependent on the concentration of the B2 salt in the synthesis. Moreover, the as-prepared carbon dots show both nitrogen and phosphorus doped characteristic, which is beneficial for their luminescent property. Besides the water soluble carbon dots recovered from the supernatant, there are also interesting carbon-coated metal particles recovered from the precipitates. Such metal-loaded carbon particles might have great potential applications in the catalysis field.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based materials have attracted great interest in the past few years due to their wide applications, including materials for super capacitors, hydrogen storage, water purification, catalysts, electrode, fluorescent probes and adsorbents [1–4]. Among others, carbon dots and carbon-coated metal particles are two kinds of important carbon-based materials which have gained much more attention.

Carbon dots have been considered as the promising candidates to replace the traditional semiconducting quantum dots for the application in biomedical area because they possess small size, excellent photoluminescence, and good biocompatibility [5]. Especially, the photoluminescence property of carbon dots is size and excitation wavelength dependent [6]. Synthesis of carbon dots using nontoxic chemicals and environmentally friendly solvents is the key issue for a green synthetic strategy. Specifically, the nontoxic carbon precursor is the most important for the synthesis of carbon dots for the safe application in the biomedical area. Although carbonization of glucose, sucrose, citric acid, etc., has been explored for the production of fluorescent carbon dots, most of the concerned processes involve multi-step operations and strong acids as well as post-treatments with surface passivated agents [7–9]. The heteroatom doping can alter intrinsic properties of carbon dots such as electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical features, which effectively modulate their photoluminescence properties [4]. However, additional chemicals (supply nitrogen and phosphorus heteroatoms) are usually needed in the fabrication of the heteroatom-doped carbon dots upon the open literatures [10–12], which increases the toxic risk from the multi-chemicals. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a one-pot green synthesis strategy of carbon dots based on a nontoxic carbon source precursor which itself contains the necessary heteroatoms like nitrogen and phosphorus for the multi-heteroatom doping.

Carbon-coated metals with core–shell structures are of great interest due to their broad applications in biosensor, fuel cell, catalyst, etc. [13–15]. Until now, various routes including the most popular arc-discharge technique have been reported for the synthesis of the carbon-coated metals [16]. However, many of the reported methods usually require toxic precursors, organic solvent, vacuum system or sophisticated procedures [17]. Therefore, the development of simple and environmentally friendly methods for the production of carbon-coated metal materials is still of great interest.

Herein, we report a one-pot green synthesis of carbon dots and carbon-coated metal (Au, Pt, Pd) particles by using the vitamin B2 salt (riboflavin 5′-monophosphate sodium salt hydrate) as both the carbon source and the reducing agent. After the hydrothermal heating of the aqueous solution of B2 salt and metal salts, the successful formed carbon dots are recovered from the supernatant while the interesting carbon-coated metal particles are recovered from the precipitates. Here, the B2 salt was chosen as the favorite precursor (carbon source and the reducing agent) which has some unique advantages: (1) It is biodegradable and nontoxic, which verifies the green formation of completely nontoxic carbon-based materials. (2) It has high water solubility which is helpful for the homogeneous formation of the carbon-based materials. (3) It has heteroatoms such as nitrogen and phosphorus which is beneficial for the luminescence quality of the formed carbon dots.

HAuCl4·3H2O, H2PtCl6·6H2O, K2PdCl4 and vitamin B2 salt (riboflavin 5′-monophosphate sodium salt hydrate) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Dialysis membrane (MWCO=1000Da) was purchased from Solarbio. Quinine sulfate was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and carbon-coated metal particles
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, the mole ratio of metal salt to vitamin B2 salt is controlled at 1:1, 1:10, 1:50, respectively. In a typical synthesis with the condition of the mole ratio between platinum salt and B2 salt at 1:10, 0.1 mmol B2 salt was added into 2g water which containing 0.01mmol H2PtCl6·6H2O under continuous stirring to result in the homogeneous solution. Then, the resultant homogeneous solution was put into a teflon-lined autoclave. After this, the autoclave was sealed and heated at 180°C for 12h. The autoclave was then cooled to room temperature naturally. Finally, the resultant products can be divided into two parts after the centrifugation. One part is the brown–yellow supernatant which contains the carbon dots. This supernatant was further subjected to dialysis against pure water through a membrane (MWCO=1000Da) to remove impurities, which results in the pure carbon dots. The other part is the precipitate which corresponds to the carbon-coated Pt particles. The precipitate was washed several times with water and ethanol, and dried at 60°C for 5h. The procedure for the synthesis of carbon dots and carbon-coated Pt particles at other mole ratios (1:1, 1:50) of platinium salt to B2 salt is similar to the above process. Moreover, we also synthesized carbon-coated Au particles and carbon-coated Pd particles based on the above procedure by using HAuCl4·3H2O and K2PdCl4 as the metal salt.

X-ray diffraction was performed using a Rigaku Dmax-rc X-ray diffractometer with Ni-filtered Cu Kα (λ =1.5418Å) radiation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEM 1400 TEM operating at 120KV. UV–vis spectra were recorded on a U-4100 spectrometer. Fluorescence spectroscopy measurements were carried out on an LS-55 spectrofluorometer. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were measured by an ALPHA-T spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried on an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer.

The quantum yield (QY) of carbon dots was measured according to the established procedure [18]. The quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (quantum yield: 0.54 at 360nm) was chosen as the reference. Then UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra (with 360nm excitation) of carbon dots and the quinine sulfate reference were measured, respectively. To minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance values of the individual solutions in 10mm cuvettes were maintained under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength. The QY value was calculated according to the following equation:
QY s
=
QY r
I s
A r
η s
2
I r
A s
η r
2
where QY is the quantum yield. s and r refer to the carbon dots sample and the quinine sulfate reference. I is the measured integrated emission intensity. η is the refractive index of the solvent while A is the UV–vis absorption intensity.

Carbon-based materials can be easily synthesized by hydrothermal heating of the aqueous solution of platinum salt and B2 salt. Carbon dots can be recovered from the supernatant of the final products while the carbon-coated Pt particles can be recovered from the precipitates. The morphology and particle size of the carbon dots were examined by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1 a shows the typical TEM image of the carbon dots recovered with the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt at 1:10. It can be seen that the formed carbon dots are spherical particles. By measuring the size of the counted carbon dots in the TEM image, the particle size distribution histogram is obtained as shown in Fig. 1b. The size of the as-prepared carbon dots is 6.4±1.5nm.

Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are further performed to understand the compositions and structures of the as-prepared carbon dots. Fig. 2 a shows the typical FTIR spectrum of the as-prepared carbon dots. The FTIR spectrum exhibits typical absorption peaks at 3424cm−1 for OH and NH, 2922cm−1 for CH, 1651cm−1 for CO, 1616cm−1 for CO, 1385cm−1 for CN, and 1050cm−1 for PO, which verifies the existence of various functional groups in the synthesized carbon dots [3,4,19,20]. XPS characterization is also studied to get more information about the composition of the as-prepared carbon dots. Fig. 2(b–e) shows the high resolution XPS spectra and the corresponding deconvoluted spectra for C1s, O1s, N1s, and P2p of the carbon dots. Based on the XPS results, it is calculated that the atomic percentage of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and phosphorus in the carbon dots is 56.20%, 32.33%, 6.76% and 4.72%, respectively. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum shows four peaks at 284.3eV, 285.0eV, 285.9eV and 287.3eV which correspond to CC/CC, CN, CO and CO groups, respectively [7]. The deconvoluted O1s spectrum shows two peaks at 531.4eV and 532.6eV which correspond to CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [7]. The deconvoluted N1s spectrum shows a peak at 400.1eV corresponding to pyrrolic/pyridinic nitrogen groups while the deconvoluted P2p spectrum shows a peak at 133.9eV corresponding to PO group [4]. Therefore, the results from FTIR and XPS clearly indicate the successful incorporation of heteroatoms such as nitrogen and phosphorus in the as-prepared carbon dots.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared carbon dots are further studied. Fig. 3 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared carbon dots. The peak around 231nm and the absorbance bands from 271nm to 400nm can be attributed to the presence of π orbitals of carbon dots [8,21]. The photograph in the insert of Fig. 3a is the image of the carbon dots solution under the illumination of daylight and UV lamp of 365nm. It is clear that the carbon dots solution can generate a blue color under the illumination of UV lamp. The emission properties of the carbon dots solution are further studied at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3b). When the excitation wavelength is from 300nm to 380nm, the emission peak position is almost unchanged with the peak centered at around 439nm. However, the emission peak shows red shifting from 439nm to 530nm by changing the excitation wavelength from 380nm to 420nm. When the excitation wavelength is further changed from 420nm to 500nm, the emission peak position is again almost unchanged with the peak centered at around 530nm. By using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield (QY) of the carbon dots was calculated to be 5.2%. The quantum yield is compared to the previously reported carbon dots. The QY of the present carbon dots is lower than some of the previous reported carbon dots. For example, the present QY is lower than the carbon dots synthesized with the surface passivated by oligomeric PEG molecules (20%) [22] and lower than the oil-soluble carbon dots synthesized with 1-hexadecylamine as the surface passivation agent (53%) [23]. Besides, the QY of the present synthesized carbon dots is also higher than some of the previous reported carbon dots. For example, the present QY is higher than the carbon dots synthesized with saccharide and base (1.2%) [24], higher than the carbon dots synthesized from natural gas soot (0.43%) [25] and higher than carbon dots synthesized from PEG (1.95%) [26]. Until now, the exact mechanism of the unique fluorescence property of carbon dots has not yet been clearly reported. The acceptable explanation of the fluorescence property is related to the particle sizes and the distribution of different emissive trap sites of the carbon dots [27,28]. The fluorescence property of our as-prepared carbon dots thus can be attributed to the presence of isolated sp2 carbon clusters and defect sites within the carbon matrix [6]. The sp2 carbon clusters with conjugated double bonds can strongly influence the band gap and optical property in the UV–vis region, enhancing the fluorescence property [29]. In the as-prepared carbon dots, the presence of heteroatoms like oxygen, nitrogen and phosphorus results in more defect sites in the sp2 carbon clusters, thus further improving the fluorescence property.

In order to understand the influence of B2 salt concentration on the morphology and size of the synthesized carbon dots, we performed experiments at different B2 salt concentrations. In all the synthesis experiments, the concentration of platinum salt is the same. Fig. 4 shows TEM images of carbon dots obtained with the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt at 1:1 and 1:50. It can be seen that the synthesized carbon dots are all spherical particles. However, the size of the obtained carbon dots is different. Based on the particle size distribution histograms in Fig. 4, the size of the carbon dots obtained at 1:1 and 1:50 is 2.8±0.6nm and 9.9±2.2nm, respectively. Combining the results of Figs. 1 and 4, it indicates the concentration of the B2 salt could significantly influence the particle size of the as-prepared carbon dots. This is easily understood because the reactant concentration is usually an important factor which governs the growth kinetics of the nanoparticles. It is clear that the size of the carbon dots increases with the concentration of the B2 salt. During the carbonizing process of the B2 salt at hydrothermal condition, the higher concentration of B2 can supply more carbon materials for the further growth of the carbon dots, therefore the higher concentration of B2 salt result in larger size of the carbon dots.

The fluorescence properties of the as-prepared carbon dots obtained with the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt at 1:1 and 1:50 are also studied. Fig. 5 (a and b) shows the emission properties of the carbon dots solution at various excitation wavelengths. For the carbon dots synthesized at 1:1, it is found the sample exhibits obvious excitation-dependent emission spectra at the excitation wavelength from 360nm to 500nm (Fig. 5a). It is clear that the emission peaks show red shifts from 468nm to 550nm at the excitation over 360–500nm. For the carbon dots synthesized at 1:50, the emission peak position shows apparent red shifting from to 446nm to 552nm with the increase of the excitation wavelength from 380nm to 500nm (Fig. 5b). The quantum yield of the carbon dots synthesized at 1:1 and 1:50 was calculated to be 4.1% and 3.8%, respectively. Comparing the result of Fig. 3 with that of Fig. 5, it is obvious that the fluorescence properties of the synthesized carbon dots are different from each others. For the carbon dots synthesized at 1:10, the emission peak position remains almost unchanged for the excitation wavelength over 300–380nm, and over 420–500nm, respectively. However, the emission peak shifted gradually with increasing the excitation wavelength for the carbon dots synthesized at 1:1 and 1:50. It is reported that different energy levels associated with different “surface states” formed by different functional groups are responsible for the excitation-dependent-emission phenomenon [8,30]. Therefore the observed excitation-independent emission over 300–380nm and over 420–500nm for the carbon dots synthesized at 1:10 indicates the carbon dots synthesized at 1:10 have a much more uniform surface than the carbon dots synthesized at 1:1 and 1:50. Moreover, the size and the various defect sites in the sp2 carbon clusters resulting from oxygen, nitrogen and phosphorus heteroatoms of the as-prepared carbon dots are also responsible for the resultant various fluorescence properties [6].

Besides the carbon dots recovered from the supernatant, we also characterize the carbon-coated Pt particles recovered from the precipitates of the final products. Here we characterized the precipitates which are formed with the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt at 1:1, 1:10 and 1:50. Fig. 6 a shows the typical XRD pattern of the carbon-coated Pt particles synthesized with the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt at 1:10. The diffraction peaks can be indexed as the face-centered cubic (fcc) Pt (JCPDS card, 65-2868). The broad peak at around 23° could be attributed the carbon materials that coated the Pt particles. The XRD results confirm the successful formation of Pt particles with highly crystallization in carbon materials. It is known that vitamin B2 could be used as an efficient reducing agent [31], therefore the successful formation of Pt particles could be easily understood when using vitamin B2 salt as the reducing agent. As the reducing agent, vitamin B2 salt is superior to vitamin B2 because of its higher solubility in water. To understand the morphologies of the synthesized particles, TEM was used for further characterization. Fig. 6(b–f) shows the corresponding low and higher magnification TEM image of the carbon-coated Pt products synthesized at 1:1, 1:10 and 1:50, respectively. From Fig. 6b, it is found that aggregated particles formed at 1:1. By close viewing the higher magnification TEM image in Fig. 6c, it is clear that these aggregated particles are constructed by nano-sized Pt particles, which are embedded in a thin layer of carbon support. The average size of the Pt particles is about 3.8±1.1nm based on the TEM image. When the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt is changed into 1:10, it still forms aggregated particles as shown in Fig. 6d. Fig. 6e shows the higher magnification TEM image of the aggregated particle which clearly verifies its detailed nanostructure with Pt nanoparticles as the subunits. The average size of the Pt is about 2.3±0.5nm. When the mole ratio of platinum salt to B2 salt becomes 1:50, Pt nanoparticle-embedded carbon particles also form (Fig. 6f). The average size of the Pt is about 3.9±0.7nm. Therefore, the present synthetic route could be a useful way for the synthesis of carbon-coated Pt particles. Because of the large size of these aggregated particles with the unique structure of Pt nanoparticles as the subunits, it is easy the separate from the solution. These carbon-coated Pt particles could precipitate themselves to the solution quickly without the aid of centrifugation. Therefore, these particles can be applied as efficient catalysts for various heterogeneous catalysis reactions with easily separation for recycle use. The formation process of the carbon-coated Pt particles and carbon dots can be illustrated as follows. During the hydrothermal process, the B2 salt reduces the platinum salt to form Pt nanoparticles at the initial stage. With the time prolonged, the B2 salt in the solution is further changed into carbon-based materials through the aromatization and carbonization. On the one hand, the initial formed Pt particles can work as the nuclei for the further growth of the carbon materials on the Pt particles. During this growth process, some formed carbon/Pt composite particles will aggregate and merge into particles for decreasing the surface energy, resulting in the carbon-coated Pt particles which finally precipitate on the bottom of the solution. On the other hand, the carbon dots can be obtained at the supersaturation point in the supernatant.

In order to understand whether the present synthesis route could be applied to the fabrication of other kinds of carbon-coated metal particles, the synthesis of carbon-coated gold and carbon-coated palladium with the mole ratio of metal salt to B2 salt at 1:1, 1:10 and 1:50 is also investigated. The size of the carbon dots synthesized by using gold salt or palladium salt is similar to those obtained by using platinum salt at the same concentration of B2 salt. Therefore, we will not further discuss the carbon dots obtained by using gold salt or palladium salt here. Fig. 7 shows the XRD and TEM results of the as-prepared carbon-coated gold and carbon-coated palladium particles. Fig. 7a shows the typical XRD patterns of the carbon-coated Au and carbon-coated Pd particles at 1:10. Typical diffraction peaks belonging to the fcc Au (JCPDS card, 04-0784) and fcc Pd (JCPDS card, 05-0681) can be indexed from Fig. 7a. The broad peak at about 23° could be attributed the carbon materials. The XRD results thus demonstrate the successful formation of the metal (Au or Pd)/carbon composites. Fig. 7(b–h) shows the corresponding low and higher magnification TEM image of the carbon-coated Au and carbon-coated Pd products, respectively. From the typical TEM images in Fig. 7(b and c), it is clear that carbon-coated Au particles form, which shows a well-defined core-shell structure at 1:1. The average size of Au core particles is about 121±35nm. Similar to the particles from at 1:1, carbon-coated Au particles with core–shell structure also form at 1:10 with the average size of Au core in 98±40nm (Fig. 7d). With the increase of mole ration to 1:50, large particles with the size of several micrometers form (Fig. 7e). From the TEM images in Fig. 7(f–h), it also shows that the carbon-coated Pd particles are formed. The average size of the synthesized Pd particles is about 15±4nm, 16±3nm and 21±4nm for the samples recovered at 1:1, 1:10, 1:50, respectively. Overall, the size of the metal particles is dependent on the mole ratio of B2 salt and metal salt. This can be explained as follows. The B2 salt works as the reducing agent for the formation of the metal particles. In this case, different mole ratios influence the reduction rate of the metal salt, thus changing the growth kinetics of the nanoparticles. Therefore, Au or Pd particles with different size form at various mole ratios. The different size between Au and Pd particles synthesized at the same mole ratio can be attributed to the crystal habit of the Au and Pd themselves. Therefore, the present synthesis strategy could be used as a general way to synthesize various kinds of carbon-coated particles.

In this contribution, we proposed a one-pot green synthesis of carbon dots and carbon-coated metal (Au, Pt, Pd) particles by using the vitamin B2 salt as the carbon source and the reducing agent. The water soluble carbon dots in supernatant and carbon-coated metal particles in the precipitates can be obtained simultaneously by heating of the aqueous solution of B2 salt and metal salts. Thanks to the component of vitamin B2 salt, the as-prepared carbon dots show both nitrogen and phosphorus doped characteristic. The obtained carbon dots have uniform diameter with small size combined with good photoluminescence property. Compared with other synthesized strategies for the carbon dots with doped heteroatoms, the present way is much safer and simple because of the biodegradable and nontoxic nature of the vitamin B2 salt without additional chemicals and step for the heteroatom doping. Besides the carbon dots, this method could also result in carbon-coated metal particles. These metal loaded carbon particle might have great potential applications in the catalysis field or others because of the metal nanoparticles as the subunits.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21173127), and the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University (Grant No. 2015JC003).

Carbon-coated core shell structured copper and nickel nanoparticles synthesized in an ionic liquid
